title: Getting Started with hurdlebb
text: Many childcare datasets share common structure: large fraction providers serve particular age group , vary widely many children enrol. Standard regression models struggle combination excess zeros bounded, overdispersed counts. hurdlebb package provides principled solution Hurdle Beta-Binomial model — two-part framework separates decision participate intensity participation. vignette learn : Explore childcare enrolment dataset recognize two-part model needed. Specify Hurdle Beta-Binomial model using hbb() formula interface. Interpret model summary, paying special attention covariate can push opposite directions across two margins. Diagnose convergence MCMC trace plots. Evaluate model fit posterior predictive checks (PPC). Compare models using approximate leave-one-cross-validation (LOO). Extract fitted values residuals diagnostics. MCMC sampler can take several minutes, vignette uses pre-computed results ship package. Every fitting command shown can reproduce analysis machine, displayed output comes saved objects.

title: Getting Started with hurdlebb
text: package includes nsece_synth_small, synthetic dataset modelled 2019 National Survey Early Care Education (NSECE). contains 504 centre-based childcare providers drawn 51 states (including DC). row provider. key columns : Two features dataset motivate Hurdle Beta-Binomial approach. First, substantial share centres serve infants toddlers : Second, among centres serve children, enrolment share varies considerably: combination — spike zero plus dispersed distribution (0,1](0, 1] — hallmark hurdle problem. plots make pattern vivid. enrolment distribution. spike zero (blue) reflects centres serve infants toddlers. Positive counts (red) show enrolment distribution among participating centres. share among serving centres. Beta-like distribution motivates Beta-Binomial intensive margin.

title: Getting Started with hurdlebb
text: Hurdle Beta-Binomial decomposes outcome two parts: Part 1 (Extensive margin): Bernoulli model whether centre serves children . participation probability qiq_i linked covariates logit: logit(qi)=Xi′α\text{logit}(q_i) = X_i'\alpha. Part 2 (Intensive margin): zero-truncated Beta-Binomial number children enrolled, conditional serving least one. expected share μi\mu_i also linked covariates logit: logit(μi)=Xi′β\text{logit}(\mu_i) = X_i'\beta, dispersion parameter κ\kappa. hurdlebb, specify margins single formula. left-hand side uses y | trials(n_trial) syntax declare count upper bound: set predictors (poverty urban) enters extensive-margin equation (coefficients α\alpha) intensive-margin equation (coefficients β\beta). shared-covariate design makes straightforward compare whether variable pushes participation intensity opposite directions.
code: # This is the code you would run (takes ~2-4 minutes): fit <- hbb(   y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban,   data   = nsece_synth_small,   chains = 4,   seed   = 42 )

title: Getting Started with hurdlebb
text: parameters fall three groups: Extensive-margin coefficients (α1,α2,α3\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3): Govern log-odds serving children. positive α\alpha means covariate increases probability participation. Intensive-margin coefficients (β1,β2,β3\beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_3): Govern log-odds share among servers. positive β\beta means covariate raises expected fraction capacity devoted . Dispersion (logκ\log\kappa): Controls extra-binomial variation. Larger κ\kappa means less overdispersion. can extract coefficients programmatically: select single margin:
code: results$coef_both #> alpha_(Intercept)     alpha_poverty       alpha_urban  beta_(Intercept)  #>        0.49674813       -0.08263571        0.10699877        0.00201966  #>      beta_poverty        beta_urban         log_kappa  #>        0.08204432        0.02102320        1.60996032 results$coef_ext   # extensive margin (alpha) #> alpha_(Intercept)     alpha_poverty       alpha_urban  #>        0.49674813       -0.08263571        0.10699877 results$coef_int   # intensive margin (beta) #> beta_(Intercept)     beta_poverty       beta_urban  #>       0.00201966       0.08204432       0.02102320

title: Getting Started with hurdlebb
text: striking pattern poverty covariate. signs opposite across two margins: Higher neighbourhood poverty associated lower probability serving children (α<0\alpha < 0), yet among centres serve, higher poverty associated larger share (β>0\beta > 0). poverty reversal central substantive finding: poverty discourages entry care intensifies enrolment among participate. standard single-equation model blend two opposing forces one ambiguous coefficient. hurdle framework lets us see margin clearly.
code: cat(sprintf("Extensive margin (alpha_poverty): %+.3f\n",             results$coef_both["alpha_poverty"])) #> Extensive margin (alpha_poverty): -0.083 cat(sprintf("Intensive margin  (beta_poverty): %+.3f\n",             results$coef_both["beta_poverty"])) #> Intensive margin  (beta_poverty): +0.082

title: Getting Started with hurdlebb
text: Convergence tells us sampler working, whether model fits data. Posterior predictive checks (PPC) compare replicated data posterior observations two summary statistics: Zero rate — fraction providers zero enrolment. Targets extensive margin. Mean share — average y / n among servers. Targets intensive margin. Posterior predictive checks. Left: replicated zero rates observed value (dashed line). Right: replicated mean shares. observed values fall within predictive distributions. observed statistics fall comfortably within respective 95% predictive intervals, confirming model captures zero-inflation intensity distribution simultaneously.

text: evaluate --sample predictive accuracy, use Pareto-smoothed importance sampling LOO (PSIS-LOO): Two quantities matter: ELPD (expected log predictive density): higher (less negative) better. useful comparing two models. Pareto kk diagnostics: observations k>0.7k > 0.7 deserve scrutiny; many threshold indicate unreliable LOO approximation.

title: Survey Design Corrections
text: large-scale social surveys draw observations random population. Instead, use complex survey designs — stratification, clustering, unequal probability sampling — make data collection feasible introduce dependencies analyst must account . Ignoring survey design leads two problems: Biased point estimates. groups oversampled, raw sample means estimate population quantities without reweighting. Underestimated standard errors. Clustering means observations within primary sampling unit (PSU) similar simple random sample produce. Naive standard errors can far small. vignette shows hurdlebb addresses problems. end understand: pass survey design variables (weights, stratum, psu) hbb(). resulting pseudo-posterior targets population parameters produces standard deviations unreliable inference. sandwich variance estimator recovers design-consistent standard errors. read design effect ratios (DERs) tell . Wald confidence intervals replace posterior intervals analysing survey data. assume read Getting Started vignette comfortable basic hbb() workflow.

title: Survey Design Corrections
text: synthetic dataset nsece_synth_small mimics 2019 National Survey Early Care Education (NSECE), nationally representative study uses stratified, clustered design: Strata group providers geography provider type sampling. Stratification improves precision ensuring every subpopulation represented. Primary sampling units (PSUs) geographic clusters (roughly counties) within stratum. Providers inside PSU share local economic regulatory conditions, creating positive intra-cluster correlation. Sampling weights equal inverse provider’s selection probability, adjusted non-response. provider weight 50 represents roughly 50 providers population. Distribution survey weights. right skew indicates providers represent many population units others.

title: Survey Design Corrections
text: Incorporating survey design hbb() requires three additional arguments — weights, stratum, psu — taking name corresponding column data frame: Behind scenes, hbb() normalises weights (sum sample size NN), multiplies provider’s log-likelihood contribution weight. result weighted pseudo-posterior shifts point estimates toward population-representative values. Note Weighted label — confirms survey weights active estimation.
code: # This is the code you would run (takes ~2-5 minutes): fit_w <- hbb(   y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban,   data    = nsece_synth_small,   weights = "weight",   stratum = "stratum",   psu     = "psu",   chains  = 4,   seed    = 42 ) results  <- readRDS(find_extdata("vig02_results.rds")) sandwich <- readRDS(find_extdata("vig02_sandwich.rds")) wald_ci  <- readRDS(find_extdata("vig02_wald_ci.rds")) loo_comp <- readRDS(find_extdata("vig02_loo_compare.rds")) cat(paste(results$print_text, collapse = "\n")) #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model Fit #> ============================== #>  #>   Model type   : Weighted (no random slopes, survey-weighted)  #>   Stan model   : hbb_weighted  #>   Formula      : y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban  #>  #>   Observations (N) : 504  #>   Covariates   (P) : 3 (intercept + 2 predictors)  #>   Zero rate        : 0.379  #>   Survey weights   : yes  #>  #> MCMC: #>   Chains           : 4  #>   Warmup           : 1000  #>   Sampling         : 1000  #>   Total draws      : 4000  #>   Elapsed          : 5.0 seconds  #>  #> Diagnostics: #>   Divergent transitions : 0  [OK]  #>   Max treedepth hits    : 0  [OK]  #>   E-BFMI                : 1.105, 1.107, 1.028, 1.062  #>   Max Rhat              : 1.0033  [OK]  #>   Min bulk ESS          : 1946  [OK]  #>   Min tail ESS          : 1951  [OK]  #>  #> Use summary() for parameter estimates.

title: Survey Design Corrections
text: standard Bayesian analysis posterior π(θ∣y)∝π(θ)∏=1NLi(θ).\pi(\theta \mid y) \propto \pi(\theta) \prod_{=1}^{N} L_i(\theta). pseudo-log-likelihood replaces ℓ̃(θ)=∑=1Nw̃ilogLi(θ),\tilde{\ell}(\theta) = \sum_{=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_i \, \log L_i(\theta), w̃=wi/w‾\tilde{w}_i = w_i / \bar{w} normalised weights. Providers representing population units contribute proportionally likelihood surface. exactly want point estimation: posterior means target finite-population parameters. However, posterior standard deviations longer trustworthy inference. weights distort curvature likelihood — provider weight 50 acts though replicated 50 times, artificially inflating effective sample size. pseudo-posterior becomes concentrated. Key insight: pseudo-posterior gives good point estimates misleading uncertainty. need sandwich variance estimator recover reliable standard errors.

title: Survey Design Corrections
text: sandwich variance estimator long history frequentist survey statistics. hurdlebb adapts pseudo-posterior setting following Williams & Savitsky (2021): Vsand=H−1JclusterH−1V_{\text{sand}} = H^{-1} \, J_{\text{cluster}} \, H^{-1} : HH observed Fisher information (negative Hessian pseudo-log-posterior). inverse H−1H^{-1} “bread” — variance get simple random sample. title: Survey Design Corrections
text: design effect ratio (DER) quantifies much survey design inflates parameter’s variance relative simple random sample: DERp=Vsand,ppHpp−1.\text{DER}_p = \frac{V_{\text{sand},pp}}{H^{-1}_{pp}}. DER 1.0 means survey design impact. DER 5.0 means true variance five times larger naive analysis suggest — equivalently, naive standard error 5≈2.24\sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 times small. Design effect ratios parameter. Design effect ratios (DER) parameter. dashed line DER = 1 marks design effect. Intercepts show greatest inflation; dispersion parameter largely unaffected. Several patterns worth noting: Intercepts largest DERs (6.84 extensive, 6.22 intensive). common: overall mean sensitive clustering every unit within cluster shares local baseline. Slope coefficients moderate DERs (1.38 4.65). Within-cluster variation covariates partially offsets clustering effect. dispersion parameter (logκ\log\kappa) smallest DER (1.17), overdispersion driven within-unit variability, clustering. mean DER across seven parameters 3.62. guideline: DERs 2–3 indicate ignoring survey design lead materially overconfident inference.
code: der_df <- data.frame(   parameter = sandwich$param_labels,   DER       = round(sandwich$DER, 2),   SE_ratio  = round(sqrt(sandwich$DER), 2) ) knitr::kable(der_df, align = c("l", "r", "r"),              caption = "Design effect ratios by parameter.") if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(ggplot2)   der_plot <- data.frame(     parameter = factor(sandwich$param_labels,                        levels = sandwich$param_labels[order(sandwich$DER)]),     DER       = sandwich$DER,     margin    = ifelse(grepl("^alpha", sandwich$param_labels), "Extensive",                 ifelse(grepl("^beta",  sandwich$param_labels), "Intensive",                        "Dispersion"))   )   der_plot$margin <- factor(der_plot$margin,                             levels = c("Extensive", "Intensive", "Dispersion"))    ggplot(der_plot, aes(x = DER, y = parameter, fill = margin)) +     geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.65) +     geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40",                linewidth = 0.6) +     geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", DER)),               hjust = -0.15, size = 3.3, colour = "grey20") +     scale_fill_manual(       values = c(Extensive = pal["extensive"], Intensive = pal["intensive"],                  Dispersion = pal["dispersion"]),       name = "Margin"     ) +     scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +     labs(x = "Design Effect Ratio (DER)", y = NULL,          title = "Design Effect Ratios") +     theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +     theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),           panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),           legend.position = "bottom") } #> Warning: No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual scale and the #> data's fill values. #> No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual scale and the #> data's fill values. #> No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual scale and the #> data's fill values.

Compare two: Wald CI table compute_wald_ci(): Wald 95% confidence intervals sandwich variance. forest plot makes comparison visual. parameter, thin line shows posterior credible interval thick translucent line shows wider Wald confidence interval: Forest plot comparing posterior (thin lines, open circles) Wald (thick translucent lines, filled triangles) 95% intervals. Wald intervals consistently wider, reflecting true sampling variability complex survey design. pattern striking. alpha_poverty, posterior standard error 0.089 sandwich standard error 0.191 — twice large, consistent DER 4.65 (4.65≈2.16\sqrt{4.65} \approx 2.16). Using posterior interval alone, might conclude poverty statistically significant negative effect participation. Wald interval, properly accounts survey design, much wider includes zero.

title: State-Varying Coefficients
text: previous vignettes treated relationship covariates enrolment constant across 51 states. assumption convenient unrealistic. States differ childcare regulations, subsidy structures, labour markets, demographics. differences systematic, pooling states together can mask important heterogeneity — produce “national average” describes state accurately. State-varying coefficients (SVC) address allowing state regression coefficients margins. Instead single poverty slope, estimate global average plus state-specific deviation. States data pull toward local evidence; states sparse data partially pooled toward national mean. vignette learn : Understand hierarchical model structure behind SVC. Specify SVC model using random-effects formula syntax hbb(). Interpret fixed effects alongside variance components. Examine random-effect standard deviations (τ\tau). Read cross-margin correlation matrix. Visualise state-level deviations caterpillar plots. Compare SVC model base model via LOO-CV. assume familiarity basic hbb() workflow Getting Started vignette.

title: State-Varying Coefficients
text: state-varying coefficient model extends base Hurdle Beta-Binomial adding state-level random effects margins. Let s()s() denote state provider ii. linear predictors become: Extensive margin (participation): logit(qi)=Xi′α+Xi′δs(α)\text{logit}(q_i) = X_i'\alpha + X_i'\delta_s^{(\alpha)} Intensive margin (enrolment share): logit(μi)=Xi′β+Xi′δs(β)\text{logit}(\mu_i) = X_i'\beta + X_i'\delta_s^{(\beta)} α\alpha β\beta global fixed effects — national-average coefficients. vectors δs=(δs(α),δs(β))\delta_s = (\delta_s^{(\alpha)}, \delta_s^{(\beta)}) state-level deviations shift state’s coefficients away national mean. effective coefficient state ss : αs=α+δs(α),βs=β+δs(β).\alpha_s = \alpha + \delta_s^{(\alpha)}, \qquad   \beta_s = \beta + \delta_s^{(\beta)}. random effects follow multivariate normal distribution: δs∼MVN(0,Σ),s=1,…,S,\delta_s \sim \text{MVN}(0, \Sigma), \qquad s = 1, \ldots, S, Σ\Sigma 2P×2P2P \times 2P covariance matrix (PP = number covariates including intercept). diagonal Σ\Sigma tells us much coefficient varies across states. -diagonal elements capture correlations, including cross-margin correlations — instance, whether states poverty encourages participation states poverty raises intensity. ensure positive-definiteness, model uses Cholesky decomposition: Σ=diag(τ)LL′diag(τ)\Sigma = \text{diag}(\tau) \, L L' \, \text{diag}(\tau), τ\tau contains standard deviations LL lower-triangular Cholesky factor LKJ prior. standard approach Stan brms.

code: if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(ggplot2)    re_df <- results$random_effects_df   fe    <- results$summary_obj$fixed_effects    # Extract poverty random effects for each margin   pov_ext <- re_df[re_df$covariate == "poverty" & re_df$margin == "extensive",                    c("state_id", "estimate")]   names(pov_ext)[2] <- "delta_alpha"   pov_int <- re_df[re_df$covariate == "poverty" & re_df$margin == "intensive",                    c("state_id", "estimate")]   names(pov_int)[2] <- "delta_beta"   scatter_df <- merge(pov_ext, pov_int, by = "state_id")    # Global fixed effects for poverty   alpha_pov <- fe$estimate[fe$parameter == "alpha_poverty"]   beta_pov  <- fe$estimate[fe$parameter == "beta_poverty"]    # Effective state-level coefficients   scatter_df$alpha_eff <- alpha_pov + scatter_df$delta_alpha   scatter_df$beta_eff  <- beta_pov  + scatter_df$delta_beta    # Reversal: alpha_eff < 0 AND beta_eff > 0   scatter_df$pattern <- ifelse(     scatter_df$alpha_eff < 0 & scatter_df$beta_eff > 0,     "Reversal", "No reversal"   )    n_rev <- sum(scatter_df$pattern == "Reversal")   n_tot <- nrow(scatter_df)    # Axis ranges for annotation placement   x_range <- range(scatter_df$delta_alpha)   y_range <- range(scatter_df$delta_beta)    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x = delta_alpha, y = delta_beta,                          colour = pattern)) +     # Reference axes (zero in random-effect space)     geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey75",                linewidth = 0.4) +     geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey75",                linewidth = 0.4) +     # Thresholds where effective coefficient = 0     geom_vline(xintercept = -alpha_pov, linetype = "dashed",                colour = pal["extensive"], linewidth = 0.6, alpha = 0.7) +     geom_hline(yintercept = -beta_pov, linetype = "dashed",                colour = pal["intensive"], linewidth = 0.6, alpha = 0.7) +     # Points     geom_point(size = 2.8, alpha = 0.85) +     scale_colour_manual(       values = c("Reversal"    = pal["dispersion"],                  "No reversal" = "grey50"),       name = NULL     ) +     # Count of reversal states     annotate("text",              x = x_range[1] + diff(x_range) * 0.02,              y = y_range[2] - diff(y_range) * 0.02,              label = sprintf("%d / %d states\nshow reversal", n_rev, n_tot),              size = 3.8, fontface = "italic", colour = pal["dispersion"],              lineheight = 0.9, hjust = 0, vjust = 1) +     # Axis threshold labels     annotate("text",              x = -alpha_pov, y = y_range[1] - diff(y_range) * 0.08,              label = expression(alpha[eff] == 0),              size = 3, colour = pal["extensive"]) +     annotate("text",              x = x_range[2] + diff(x_range) * 0.05,              y = -beta_pov,              label = expression(beta[eff] == 0),              size = 3, colour = pal["intensive"]) +     labs(x = expression(delta[alpha] ~ "(extensive poverty random effect)"),          y = expression(delta[beta]  ~ "(intensive poverty random effect)"),          title = "Cross-Margin Poverty Effects by State") +     theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +     theme(legend.position = "bottom",           panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) } #> Warning: No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual scale and the #> data's colour values. #> No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual scale and the #> data's colour values.

Average marginal effects (AMEs) translate logit-scale parameters probability-scale changes, answering question: increase covariate xkx_k one unit every centre sample, much expected share change average? hurdle model’s two-part structure adds crucial twist. expected share product two probabilities — participation qiq_i conditional intensity μi\mu_i — single covariate can push outcome opposite directions across two margins. AME decomposition makes transparent separating covariate’s effect extensive-margin intensive-margin contributions. vignette learn : Derive AME decomposition product rule. Compute extensive, intensive, total AMEs using ame(). Read decomposition table sign patterns share percentages. Visualise poverty reversal posterior density plots. Quantify reversal probability posterior draws. Optionally obtain design-corrected Wald AME intervals via sandwich variance.

title: Marginal Effects
text: Hurdle Beta-Binomial model implies expected share centre ii : E[yini]=qi⋅μi,E\!\left[\frac{y_i}{n_i}\right] = q_i \cdot \mu_i, qi=logistic(Xi′α)q_i = \text{logistic}(X_i'\alpha) participation probability (extensive margin) μi=logistic(Xi′β)\mu_i = \text{logistic}(X_i'\beta) conditional enrolment share (intensive margin). Differentiating respect covariate xikx_{ik} using product rule: ∂E[yi/ni]∂xik=αkqi(1−qi)μi⏟extensive-margin AME+βkμi(1−μi)qi⏟intensive-margin AME.\frac{\partial\, E[y_i / n_i]}{\partial\, x_{ik}} = \underbrace{\alpha_k \, q_i(1 - q_i) \, \mu_i}_{\text{extensive-margin AME}} \;+\; \underbrace{\beta_k \, \mu_i(1 - \mu_i) \, q_i}_{\text{intensive-margin AME}}. first term captures much xkx_k changes share altering whether centre participates. second captures much xkx_k changes share altering intensely participating centres serve children. average marginal effect averages NN observations: AMEk=1N∑=1N[extik+intik]=AMEkext+AMEkint.\text{AME}_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{=1}^{N} \bigl[\text{ext}_{ik} + \text{int}_{ik}\bigr] = \text{AME}_k^{\text{ext}} + \text{AME}_k^{\text{int}}. key computational insight: NN-vectors qi(1−qi)μiq_i(1-q_i)\mu_i μi(1−μi)qi\mu_i(1-\mu_i)q_i depend covariate kk differentiating respect . compute per MCMC draw multiply appropriate coefficient: AMEkext=αk⋅q′(1−q)μ¯,AMEkint=βk⋅μ′(1−μ)q¯,\text{AME}_k^{\text{ext}} = \alpha_k \cdot \overline{q'(1-q)\,\mu}, \qquad \text{AME}_k^{\text{int}} = \beta_k \cdot \overline{\mu'(1-\mu)\,q}, reducing per-draw cost O(NP)O(NP) O(N)O(N).

title: Marginal Effects
text: row shows one-unit increase covariate changes expected share, decomposed : Ext AME: contribution participation probability (αk⋅q′(1−q)μ¯\alpha_k \cdot \overline{q'(1-q)\mu}). Int AME: contribution conditional intensity (βk⋅μ′(1−μ)q¯\beta_k \cdot \overline{\mu'(1-\mu)q}). Total AME: sum two components. Ext / Int %: component’s share total absolute magnitude. Pattern: Whether two components push direction (“reinforcing”) opposite directions (“opposing”). covariates display opposing pattern, meaning extensive intensive effects work . hallmark hurdle model ignoring two-part structure produce misleading single coefficient.

text: working complex survey data, posterior-based AME credible intervals may understate uncertainty (see Survey Design vignette). Pass cholesky sandwich arguments ame() design-corrected Wald CIs via delta method: Wald variance Var(AMEk)≈∇θAMEk(θ̂)⊤Vsand∇θAMEk(θ̂)\text{Var}(\text{AME}_k) \approx \nabla_\theta \text{AME}_k(\hat\theta)^\top V_{\text{sand}} \nabla_\theta \text{AME}_k(\hat\theta), gradient computed central differences (ϵ=10−5\epsilon = 10^{-5}). print output includes “Wald AME” section sign-agreement check posterior Wald approaches.
code: sand <- sandwich_variance(fit) chol <- cholesky_correct(fit, sand) ame_corrected <- ame(fit, cholesky = chol, sandwich = sand)

title: Marginal Effects
text: AME decomposition separates extensive intensive channels. single covariate effect split participation component (αk⋅q′(1−q)μ¯\alpha_k \cdot \overline{q'(1-q)\mu}) intensity component (βk⋅μ′(1−μ)q¯\beta_k \cdot \overline{\mu'(1-\mu)q}). decomposition unique hurdle model impossible recover single-equation specification. Opposing signs reveal hidden heterogeneity. two components push opposite directions (“opposing” “reversal” pattern), standard model report net effect masks true complexity. AME decomposition makes channels visible. reversal probability quantifies posterior certainty. Rather relying point-estimate sign comparisons, reversal probability uses full posterior distribution assess confidently can claim opposing-sign pattern holds. Design correction extends AMEs. delta-method Wald intervals, computed via sandwich variance, ensure AME inference accounts survey design. especially important design effect ratios large. Magnitude context matters. Always interpret AMEs baseline population predictions (q‾\bar{q}, μ‾\bar{\mu}). seemingly small AME can represent large proportional change relative baseline.

title: hurdlebb: Bayesian Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models
text: Two-part hurdle framework — Separates decision participate (extensive margin, Bernoulli) intensity participation (intensive margin, zero-truncated Beta-Binomial). Survey design support — Pseudo-posterior inference sampling weights, sandwich standard errors, Cholesky posterior recalibration. State-varying coefficients (SVC) — Hierarchical random intercepts slopes optional cross-level policy moderators via state_level(). Marginal effect decomposition — Average marginal effects decomposed extensive intensive margin contributions, reversal probability detection. Model comparison — PSIS-LOO cross-validation multi-model comparison Pareto-k diagnostics. Posterior predictive checks — Customizable PPC statistics evaluating model fit.

title: hurdlebb: Bayesian Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models
text: hurdle Beta-Binomial decomposes bounded count outcome two parts: Part 1 — Extensive margin (participation): Bernoulli model whether unit non-zero count. participation probability q linked covariates via logit: logit(q) = X’α. Part 2 — Intensive margin (intensity): zero-truncated Beta-Binomial count conditional positive. expected proportion μ linked covariates via logit: logit(μ) = X’β, dispersion parameter κ. margins share design matrix separate coefficient vectors (α β), making straightforward compare whether covariate pushes participation intensity opposite directions.

title: Average Marginal Effects Decomposition for Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models — ame
text: hurdle Beta-Binomial model implies two channels covariates affect expected outcome.  extensive margin governs whether center serves infant/toddler children (\(q_i = \mathrm{logistic}(X_i' \alpha)\)), intensive margin governs enrollment share conditional participation (\(\mu_i = \mathrm{logistic}(X_i' \beta)\)). product rule, marginal effect covariate \(k\) \(E[y_i / n_i]\) decomposes : $$   \frac{\partial E[y_i/n_i]}{\partial x_{ik}}     = \underbrace{\alpha_k \, q_i(1-q_i) \, \mu_i}_{\text{extensive}}     + \underbrace{\beta_k \, \mu_i(1-\mu_i) \, q_i}_{\text{intensive}}. $$ Average Marginal Effect averages \(N\) observations: $$   \mathrm{AME}_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{=1}^{N}     \bigl[\alpha_k \, q_i(1-q_i) \, \mu_i           + \beta_k \, \mu_i(1-\mu_i) \, q_i\bigr]     = \mathrm{AME}_k^{\mathrm{ext}} + \mathrm{AME}_k^{\mathrm{int}}. $$

title: Average Marginal Effects Decomposition for Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models — ame
text: sandwich provided, function also computes delta-method Wald confidence intervals AME component. numerical gradient \(\mathrm{AME}_k(\theta)\) respect \(\theta\) computed via central differences (step size \(\epsilon = 10^{-5}\)), Wald variance : $$   \mathrm{Var}(\mathrm{AME}_k)     \approx \nabla_\theta \mathrm{AME}_k(\hat\theta)^\top             V_{\mathrm{sand}}\,             \nabla_\theta \mathrm{AME}_k(\hat\theta). $$

title: Average Marginal Effects Decomposition for Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models — ame
text: computation vectorised observations within MCMC draw. key insight N-vectors \(q'\), \(\mu\), \(\mu'\), \(q\) independent covariate index \(k\), : $$   \mathrm{AME}_k^{\mathrm{ext}} = \alpha_k \cdot \overline{q'(1-q) \mu},   \quad   \mathrm{AME}_k^{\mathrm{int}} = \beta_k \cdot \overline{\mu'(1-\mu) q}, $$ reducing per-draw cost \(O(NP)\) \(O(N)\).

Cholesky correction constructs affine map $$   \theta^{*(m)} = \hat\theta + \bigl(\theta^{(m)} - \hat\theta\bigr),   \quad = L_{\mathrm{sand}}\, L_{\mathrm{MCMC}}^{-1}, $$ \(L_{\mathrm{sand}}\) \(L_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) lower-triangular Cholesky factors sandwich variance \(V_{\mathrm{sand}}\) MCMC covariance \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\), respectively.

title: Cholesky Posterior Recalibration for Survey-Weighted Inference — cholesky_correct
text: Applies affine Cholesky correction Williams Savitsky (2021, Theorem 4.1) MCMC posterior draws, replacing prior-dominated posterior covariance design-consistent sandwich variance preserving posterior mean.

title: Cholesky Posterior Recalibration for Survey-Weighted Inference — cholesky_correct
text: transformation satisfies three exact algebraic identities: Mean preservation. \(E[\theta^*] = \hat\theta\) affine map centred posterior mean. Covariance recovery. $$       \mathrm{Cov}(\theta^*) = \,\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\,^\top         = L_{\mathrm{sand}}\,L_{\mathrm{MCMC}}^{-1}\,           \Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\,           L_{\mathrm{MCMC}}^{-\top}\,L_{\mathrm{sand}}^\top         = V_{\mathrm{sand}}.     $$ middle cancellation uses \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}} = L_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\,L_{\mathrm{MCMC}}^\top\). Affine equivariance. Quantile ordering preserved monotone marginal transformations, credible intervals corrected draws valid frequentist confidence intervals correct nominal coverage.

title: Cholesky Posterior Recalibration for Survey-Weighted Inference — cholesky_correct
text: parameters prior dominates likelihood (Prior Inflation \(\mathrm{PI}_p = \Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC},pp} / H_{\mathrm{obs},pp}^{-1} \gg 1\)), diagonal \(\) much less unity (typically 0.01–0.06). shrinks -dispersed MCMC draws towards \(\hat\theta\), replacing prior-inflated credible intervals data-driven confidence intervals. expected correct behaviour: sandwich variance \(V_{\mathrm{sand}}\) smaller \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) \(H_{\mathrm{obs}}^{-1}\) design effects moderate. parameters prior contributes little (e.g., log_kappa \(\mathrm{PI} \approx 2.3\)), \(A_{pp} \approx 1.06\), producing slight inflation consistent design-effect adjustment.

title: Cholesky Posterior Recalibration for Survey-Weighted Inference — cholesky_correct
text: DER key diagnostic correction magnitude: $$   \mathrm{DER}_p = \frac{V_{\mathrm{sand},pp}}{H_{\mathrm{obs},pp}^{-1}}. $$ Values range 1–5 typical complex survey designs.

text: Extensive margin (alpha block, \(P \times P\)): analytic Fisher information logistic regression. Intensive + kappa block (\((P+1) \times (P+1)\)): empirical information identity using posterior mean scores.

title: Compute the Block-Diagonal Observed Information Matrix — compute_H_obs
text: base weighted model: $$H_{\mathrm{ext}} = \sum_{=1}^N \tilde{w}_i\, q_i (1-q_i)\,   X_i X_i^\top$$ \(q_i = \mathrm{logit}^{-1}(X_i^\top \hat{\alpha})\). SVC models, linear predictor includes state random effects: \(q_i = \mathrm{logit}^{-1}(X_i^\top \hat{\alpha} + X_i^\top \hat{\delta}^{\mathrm{ext}}_{s[]})\).

title: Compute the Block-Diagonal Observed Information Matrix — compute_H_obs
text: zero-truncated Beta-Binomial Hessian analytically complex, use information identity: $$H_{\mathrm{int}} = \sum_{=1}^N \tilde{w}_i\,   s_i^{\mathrm{int}} (s_i^{\mathrm{int}})^\top$$ \(s_i^{\mathrm{int}} = (s_{\beta,}, s_{\kappa,})^\top\) \((P+1)\)-dimensional intensive score posterior mean.

title: Compute the Cluster-Robust Meat Matrix — compute_J_cluster
text: stratum \(h\) \(C_h\) PSUs: Compute weighted score totals per PSU: \(s_{hc} = \sum_{\\mathrm{PSU}(h,c)} \tilde{w}_i s_i\) Compute stratum mean: \(\bar{s}_h = C_h^{-1} \sum_c s_{hc}\) Center: \(\delta_{hc} = s_{hc} - \bar{s}_h\) Accumulate FPC: \(J_h = \frac{C_h}{C_h - 1}    \sum_c \delta_{hc} \delta_{hc}^\top\) Uses rowsum() efficient PSU-level aggregation. title: Compute P(Y = 0) under the Beta-Binomial distribution — compute_p0
text: numeric vector probabilities \([0, 1]\).

title: Compute P(Y = 0) under the Beta-Binomial distribution — compute_p0
text: formula $$   p_0 = \frac{B(b + n,\; )}{B(b,\; )}   = \exp\bigl[\Gamma(b + n) + \ln\Gamma(\kappa)              - \ln\Gamma(b) - \ln\Gamma(\kappa + n)\bigr], $$ \(= \mu\kappa\) \(b = (1 - \mu)\kappa\). Boundary handling: \(\mu = 0\): Returns 1 (point mass zero). \(\mu = 1\): Returns 0 (point mass \(n\)). \(n = 0\): Returns 1 (trivially, trials). result clamped \([0, 1]\) guard floating-point drift.

title: Compute P(Y = 0) under the Beta-Binomial distribution — compute_p0
text: Ghosal, S., Ghosh, S., Moores, M. (2020). “Hierarchical beta-binomial models batch effects cytometry data.” Journal Royal Statistical Society: Series , 183(4), 1579–1601.

title: Probability mass function of the Beta-Binomial distribution — dbetabinom
text: PMF $$   f(y \mid n, \mu, \kappa)   = \binom{n}{y} \frac{B(y + ,\; n - y + b)}{B(, b)},   \quad y \\{0, 1, \ldots, n\}, $$ \(= \mu \kappa\) \(b = (1 - \mu) \kappa\). Boundary handling. \(\mu = 0\) Beta-Binomial degenerates point mass \(y = 0\) (probability zero). \(\mu = 1\) degenerates point mass \(y = n\). boundaries handled explicit branches lbeta formula produces NaN \(= 0\) \(b = 0\). arguments recycled common length via rep_len.

what: Probability mass function of the Zero-Truncated Beta-Binomial — dztbetabinom
title: Probability mass function of the Zero-Truncated Beta-Binomial — dztbetabinom
text: Computes PMF zero-truncated Beta-Binomial distribution \(y \\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}\).

title: Probability mass function of the Zero-Truncated Beta-Binomial — dztbetabinom
code: dztbetabinom(y, n, mu, kappa, log = FALSE)

title: Probability mass function of the Zero-Truncated Beta-Binomial — dztbetabinom
text: y Integer vector observed counts (\(y \ge 1\)). n Integer vector trial sizes (\(n \ge 1\)). mu Numeric vector means, \((\varepsilon, 1 - \varepsilon)\) \(\varepsilon\) machine epsilon. kappa Numeric vector concentrations, \(> 0\). log Logical; TRUE, return log-probabilities.

title: Probability mass function of the Zero-Truncated Beta-Binomial — dztbetabinom
text: numeric vector (log-)probabilities.

title: Probability mass function of the Zero-Truncated Beta-Binomial — dztbetabinom
text: zero-truncated PMF $$   f_{\text{ZT}}(y \mid n, \mu, \kappa)   = \frac{f_{\text{BB}}(y \mid n, \mu, \kappa)}{1 - p_0},   \quad y \\{1, \ldots, n\}, $$ \(p_0 = P(Y = 0)\) untruncated Beta-Binomial. Numerical stability ensured computing log domain using log1mexp() \(\log(1 - p_0)\).

title: Probability mass function of the Zero-Truncated Beta-Binomial — dztbetabinom
text: Ghosal, S., Ghosh, S., Moores, M. (2020). “Hierarchical beta-binomial models batch effects cytometry data.” Journal Royal Statistical Society: Series , 183(4), 1579–1601.

title: Probability mass function of the Zero-Truncated Beta-Binomial — dztbetabinom
code: # ZT-BB PMF (support starts at 1) dztbetabinom(1:5, n = 5, mu = 0.3, kappa = 10) #> [1] 0.40909091 0.32727273 0.18181818 0.06818182 0.01363636  # Sums to 1 over the support sum(dztbetabinom(1:5, n = 5, mu = 0.3, kappa = 10)) #> [1] 1  # Log scale dztbetabinom(1:5, n = 5, mu = 0.3, kappa = 10, log = TRUE) #> [1] -0.8938179 -1.1169614 -1.7047481 -2.6855773 -4.2950153

what: Fit a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb
title: Fit a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb
text: Fits two-part hurdle Beta-Binomial model using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) via CmdStan. model comprises extensive margin (Bernoulli whether center serves infant/toddler children) intensive margin (zero-truncated Beta-Binomial enrollment share among servers). margins share design matrix separate coefficient vectors.

title: Fit a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb
code: hbb(   formula,   data,   weights = NULL,   stratum = NULL,   psu = NULL,   state_data = NULL,   prior = default_prior(),   chains = 4L,   iter_warmup = 1000L,   iter_sampling = 1000L,   adapt_delta = 0.95,   max_treedepth = 12L,   seed = NULL,   refresh = NULL,   cpp_options = list(),   ... )

title: Fit a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb
text: formula two-sided formula form y | trials(n) ~ predictors, object class hbb_formula. See hbb_formula() full syntax including random effects policy moderators. data data frame containing provider-level variables: response, trials, fixed effects, optionally grouping variable. weights Character string naming column data containing survey sampling weights, NULL (default) unweighted analysis. stratum Character string naming column data containing sampling stratum identifiers, NULL. psu Character string naming column data containing primary sampling unit (PSU) identifiers, NULL. state_data data frame group-level (state-level) variables policy moderators. Required formula includes state_level() terms. Must contain column matching grouping variable merging. prior prior specification object class hbb_prior, NULL use default_prior(). See hbb_prior() customisation. chains Integer. Number Markov chains. Default 4. iter_warmup Integer. Number warmup iterations per chain. Default 1000. iter_sampling Integer. Number post-warmup iterations per chain. Default 1000. adapt_delta Numeric (0, 1). Target average proposal acceptance probability warmup adaptation. Higher values reduce divergent transitions cost slower sampling. Default 0.95. max_treedepth Integer. Maximum tree depth NUTS sampler. Default 12. seed Integer NULL. Random seed reproducibility. refresh Integer NULL. often print progress (every refresh iterations). Set 0 suppress progress output. NULL uses CmdStan default. cpp_options Named list C++ compilation options passed cmdstanr::cmdstan_model(). example, list(stan_threads = TRUE) enables within-chain threading. Default list(). ... Additional arguments passed CmdStanModel $sample() method.

title: Fit a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb
text: hbb() performs following steps: Parses formula (raw formula rather hbb_formula). Validates prior specification MCMC arguments. Prepares Stan data via prepare_stan_data(). Maps prepared data priors Stan data block. Compiles appropriate Stan model (cached first use). Runs MCMC sampling via CmdStan. Checks MCMC pathologies (divergences, treedepth, E-BFMI). Returns hbb_fit object downstream analysis.

title: Fit a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(nsece_synth_small, package = "hurdlebb")  # Base model (no weights, no random effects) fit1 <- hbb(   y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban,   data = nsece_synth_small,   chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500 ) print(fit1)  # Weighted model fit2 <- hbb(   y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban,   data = nsece_synth_small,   weights = "weight",   chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500 )  # SVC model with policy moderators data(nsece_state_policy, package = "hurdlebb") fit3 <- hbb(   y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban +     (poverty + urban | state_id) +     state_level(mr_pctile),   data = nsece_synth_small,   state_data = nsece_state_policy,   weights = "weight",   chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500 ) } # }

title: Parse a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model Formula — hbb_formula
text: S3 object class "hbb_formula" components: response Character. response variable name. trials Character. trials variable name. fixed Character vector fixed effect term names, excluding intercept (always implicit). group Character NULL. grouping variable random effects. random Character vector random effect terms, NULL random effects. Contains "1" intercept-random effects, variable names state-varying coefficients. policy Character vector policy moderator term names, NULL none specified. formula original formula object. svc Logical. TRUE model includes random slopes (state-varying coefficients), just random intercept.

title: Parse a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model Formula — hbb_formula
text: left-hand side must specify response variable trials variable using | trials() syntax:   right-hand side supports four types terms: Fixed effects: Standard R formula terms (e.g., x1 + x2). intercept always included automatically. Use y | trials(n) ~ 1 intercept-model. Random intercept: (1 | group) specifies random intercept grouped group. State-varying coefficients (SVC): (x1 + x2 | group) specifies random slopes x1 x2 (plus implicit random intercept). random slope variables must also appear fixed effects. Policy moderators: state_level(v1 + v2) specifies cross-level interaction terms predict random effects. require random effects also specified.
code: y | trials(n_trial) ~ ...

title: Parse a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model Formula — hbb_formula
text: parsed formula maps model follows: Fixed effects \(\\) \(X\) matrix (auto-prepended intercept column) grouping variable \(\\) state index vector Policy moderators \(\\) \(V\) matrix (auto-prepended intercept column) hurdle margins share \(X\) design matrix

title: Parse a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model Formula — hbb_formula
code: # Intercept-only model f1 <- hbb_formula(y | trials(n_trial) ~ 1) f1 #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Formula #> ---------------------------- #>   Response : y  #>   Trials   : n_trial  #>   Fixed    : (intercept only) #>   Random   : none #>   Policy   : none #>   SVC      : FALSE   # Fixed effects only f2 <- hbb_formula(y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban) f2 #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Formula #> ---------------------------- #>   Response : y  #>   Trials   : n_trial  #>   Fixed    : intercept + poverty + urban  #>   Random   : none #>   Policy   : none #>   SVC      : FALSE   # Random intercept f3 <- hbb_formula(y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + (1 | state_id)) f3 #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Formula #> ---------------------------- #>   Response : y  #>   Trials   : n_trial  #>   Fixed    : intercept + poverty  #>   Random   : (1 | state_id )   [random intercept] #>   Policy   : none #>   SVC      : FALSE   # SVC with policy moderators f4 <- hbb_formula(   y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban +     (poverty + urban | state_id) +     state_level(mr_pctile + tiered_reim) ) f4 #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Formula #> ---------------------------- #>   Response : y  #>   Trials   : n_trial  #>   Fixed    : intercept + poverty + urban  #>   Random   : (1 + poverty + urban | state_id )   [SVC] #>   Policy   : state_level( mr_pctile + tiered_reim ) #>   SVC      : TRUE

what: Compare Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models via LOO-CV — hbb_loo_compare
title: Compare Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models via LOO-CV — hbb_loo_compare
text: Models ranked highest (best) lowest (worst) ELPD. ELPD difference \(\Delta \mathrm{elpd}_{ij} = \mathrm{elpd}_i - \mathrm{elpd}_j\) best model model reported together standard error. consecutive pairs ranked list, pairwise z-ratio : $$   z_{AB} = \frac{\Delta\,\mathrm{elpd}_{AB}}                 {\mathrm{SE}(\Delta\,\mathrm{elpd}_{AB})}, $$ \(\mathrm{SE}\) estimated pointwise ELPD differences via: $$   \mathrm{SE}(\Delta\,\mathrm{elpd}_{AB})   = \sqrt{N} \cdot \mathrm{SD}\bigl(\hat{\ell}_i^{()}       - \hat{\ell}_i^{(B)}\bigr), $$ \(\hat{\ell}_i^{()}\) per-observation LOO log-density model \(\).  magnitude \(|z| > 2\) taken substantial evidence higher-ranked model (Vehtari et al., 2017).

title: Compare Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models via LOO-CV — hbb_loo_compare
code: hbb_loo_compare(..., cores = 1L)

title: Compare Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models via LOO-CV — hbb_loo_compare

what: Specify Priors for a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb_prior
title: Specify Priors for a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb_prior
text: Creates prior specification object controls prior distributions used Stan model. arguments optional; unspecified components receive defaults default_prior().

title: Specify Priors for a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb_prior
code: hbb_prior(   alpha = NULL,   beta = NULL,   log_kappa = NULL,   gamma = NULL,   tau = NULL,   lkj_eta = NULL )

title: Specify Priors for a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb_prior
text: alpha Prior extensive-margin fixed effects \(\alpha\) (logit scale). named list elements dist (character), mean (numeric scalar), sd (positive numeric scalar). Currently dist = "normal" supported. beta Prior intensive-margin fixed effects \(\beta\) (logit scale). structure alpha. log_kappa Prior log-concentration parameter \(\log \kappa\). structure alpha. gamma Prior policy moderator coefficients \(\Gamma\). structure alpha. tau Prior random effect scale parameters \(\tau\). structure alpha. Note: Stan, \(\tau\) declared lower = 0, Normal(mean, sd) prior becomes half-normal prior practice. mean component typically set 0 half-normal priors. lkj_eta Concentration parameter \(\eta > 0\) LKJ prior Cholesky factor correlation matrix \(L_\Omega\). Higher values shrink correlation toward identity matrix. scalar \(\eta = 1\) gives uniform prior correlation matrices; \(\eta = 2\) (default) mildly favours identity.

title: Specify Priors for a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb_prior
text: S3 object class "hbb_prior" components: alpha Named list dist, mean, sd. beta Named list dist, mean, sd. log_kappa Named list dist, mean, sd. gamma Named list dist, mean, sd. tau Named list dist, mean, sd. lkj_eta Positive numeric scalar.

title: Specify Priors for a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb_prior
text: dist field component currently accepts "normal". structure designed accommodate future distributions (e.g., Student-t) without breaking API.

title: Specify Priors for a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb_prior
text: parameters present every model variant: hbb_base hbb_weighted: use alpha, beta, log_kappa . hbb_svc hbb_svc_weighted: additionally use gamma, tau, lkj_eta. Unused prior components silently ignored fitting function.

title: Specify Priors for a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — hbb_prior
code: # Default priors default_prior() #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Prior Specification #> ---------------------------------------- #>   alpha     ~ Normal(0, 2)  #>   beta      ~ Normal(0, 2)  #>   log_kappa ~ Normal(2, 1.5)  #>   gamma     ~ Normal(0, 1)  #>   tau       ~ HalfNormal(0, 1)  #>   L_Omega   ~ LKJ(2)   # Tighter prior on fixed effects hbb_prior(alpha = list(dist = "normal", mean = 0, sd = 1)) #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Prior Specification #> ---------------------------------------- #>   alpha     ~ Normal(0, 1)  #>   beta      ~ Normal(0, 2)  #>   log_kappa ~ Normal(2, 1.5)  #>   gamma     ~ Normal(0, 1)  #>   tau       ~ HalfNormal(0, 1)  #>   L_Omega   ~ LKJ(2)   # Custom prior on dispersion and LKJ hbb_prior(   log_kappa = list(dist = "normal", mean = 3, sd = 1),   lkj_eta   = 4 ) #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Prior Specification #> ---------------------------------------- #>   alpha     ~ Normal(0, 2)  #>   beta      ~ Normal(0, 2)  #>   log_kappa ~ Normal(3, 1)  #>   gamma     ~ Normal(0, 1)  #>   tau       ~ HalfNormal(0, 1)  #>   L_Omega   ~ LKJ(4)   # Override everything p <- hbb_prior(   alpha     = list(dist = "normal", mean = 0, sd = 1),   beta      = list(dist = "normal", mean = 0, sd = 1),   log_kappa = list(dist = "normal", mean = 2, sd = 1),   gamma     = list(dist = "normal", mean = 0, sd = 0.5),   tau       = list(dist = "normal", mean = 0, sd = 0.5),   lkj_eta   = 5 ) print(p) #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Prior Specification #> ---------------------------------------- #>   alpha     ~ Normal(0, 1)  #>   beta      ~ Normal(0, 1)  #>   log_kappa ~ Normal(2, 1)  #>   gamma     ~ Normal(0, 0.5)  #>   tau       ~ HalfNormal(0, 0.5)  #>   L_Omega   ~ LKJ(5)

what: Variance under the Hurdle Beta-Binomial — hurdle_variance
title: Variance under the Hurdle Beta-Binomial — hurdle_variance
text: Computes unconditional variance via law total variance: $$   \text{Var}[Y] = q \cdot V_{\text{ZT}}                  + q (1 - q) \cdot (E_{\text{ZT}})^2, $$ \(E_{\text{ZT}} = n\mu / (1 - p_0)\) zero-truncated mean \(V_{\text{ZT}}\) zero-truncated variance.

title: Variance under the Hurdle Beta-Binomial — hurdle_variance
code: hurdle_variance(n, q, mu, kappa)

title: Variance under the Hurdle Beta-Binomial — hurdle_variance
text: n Integer vector trial sizes (\(n \ge 1\)). q Numeric vector participation probabilities, \([0, 1]\). mu Numeric vector intensity means \((\varepsilon, 1 - \varepsilon)\). kappa Numeric vector concentrations (\(> 0\)).

title: Variance under the Hurdle Beta-Binomial — hurdle_variance
text: numeric vector variances (non-negative).

title: Variance under the Hurdle Beta-Binomial — hurdle_variance
text: zero-truncated variance derived untruncated moments: $$   V_{\text{ZT}} = \frac{E_{\text{BB}}[Y^2]}{1 - p_0}                 - \left(\frac{E_{\text{BB}}[Y]}{1 - p_0}\right)^2, $$ $$   E_{\text{BB}}[Y^2] = \text{Var}_{\text{BB}} + (n\mu)^2   = n\mu(1 - \mu) \frac{n + \kappa}{1 + \kappa} + n^2 \mu^2. $$ result clamped non-negative via pmax(., 0).

title: Variance under the Hurdle Beta-Binomial — hurdle_variance
text: Ghosal, S., Ghosh, S., Moores, M. (2020). “Hierarchical beta-binomial models batch effects cytometry data.” Journal Royal Statistical Society: Series , 183(4), 1579–1601.

title: Variance under the Hurdle Beta-Binomial — hurdle_variance
code: hurdle_variance(n = 10, q = 0.7, mu = 0.3, kappa = 5) #> [1] 5.635486  # q = 0 always gives 0 hurdle_variance(n = 10, q = 0, mu = 0.3, kappa = 5) #> [1] 0

what: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation for Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models — loo.hbb_fit
title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation for Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models — loo.hbb_fit
text: PSIS-LOO approximates leave-one-predictive density: $$   p(y_i \mid \mathbf{y}_{-})   = \int p(y_i \mid \theta) \, p(\theta \mid \mathbf{y}_{-}) \, d\theta, $$ using importance sampling weights proportional \(1/p(y_i \mid \theta)\).  Raw importance weights heavy tails; PSIS stabilises fitting generalised Pareto distribution largest weights replacing upper tail. expected log predictive density (ELPD) : $$   \widehat{\mathrm{elpd}}_{\mathrm{LOO}}   = \sum_{=1}^{N} \log p(y_i \mid \mathbf{y}_{-}), $$ LOO information criterion \(\mathrm{LOOIC} = -2 \cdot \widehat{\mathrm{elpd}}_{\mathrm{LOO}}\). Models higher (less negative) ELPD predict held-data better.

title: Synthetic NSECE 2019 Center-Based Provider Data — nsece_synth
text: data-generating process uses two-part hurdle Beta-Binomial model: Part 1 (Extensive margin) Whether center serves children , modeled \(z_i \sim \textrm{Bernoulli}(q_i)\) \(\textrm{logit}(q_i) = X_i \alpha + \delta_{1,s_i}\). Part 2 (Intensive margin) Among servers, enrollment count follows zero-truncated Beta-Binomial: \(y_i \mid z_i = 1 \sim \textrm{ZT-BetaBin}(n_i, \mu_i, \kappa)\) \(\textrm{logit}(\mu_i) = X_i \beta + \delta_{2,s_i}\). structural zero rate approximately 35 percent, mean share among servers approximately 48 percent. match empirical moments original NSECE 2019 data.

title: Plot Diagnostics and Inferential Summaries for hbb_fit Objects — plot.hbb_fit
text: method dispatches four specialised internal helpers: "coefficients" forest plot fixed-effect point estimates confidence/credible intervals, faceted model margin (extensive, intensive, dispersion).  Uses summary.hbb_fit internally, inference mode (Wald posterior) applies. "trace" MCMC trace plots showing evolution sampled parameter values across iterations, coloured chain. Useful diagnosing convergence mixing. "random_effects" Caterpillar plots posterior mean credible interval state-level random effect \(\delta_{s,k}\), faceted covariate.  available SVC models (model_type %% c("svc", "svc_weighted")). "residuals" two-panel diagnostic combining residuals-versus-fitted normal QQ plot Pearson residuals.  Panels arranged via patchwork.

what: Posterior Predictive Checks for Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models — ppc
title: Posterior Predictive Checks for Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models — ppc
text: function evaluates two test statistics designed probe two structural components hurdle Beta-Binomial model: Zero rate $$T_0(\mathbf{y}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{=1}^{N}       \mathbf{1}(y_i = 0).$$ Targets extensive margin: whether center serves infant/toddler children .  fitted model, \(E[T_0(\mathbf{y}^{\mathrm{rep}})] = 1 - N^{-1}\sum_i q_i\). share $$T_s(\mathbf{y}) = \frac{1}{N^+}       \sum_{:\, y_i > 0} \frac{y_i}{n_i},$$ \(N^+ = \#\{: y_i > 0\}\) count participating providers.  Targets intensive margin: enrollment share conditional participation.  fitted model, \(E[T_s(\mathbf{y}^{\mathrm{rep}}) \mid y^{\mathrm{rep}} > 0]     \approx N^{-1}\sum_i \mu_i\).

title: Posterior Predictive Checks for Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models — ppc
text: well-calibrated Bayesian model produce posterior predictive distributions \(p(\mathbf{y}^{\mathrm{rep}} \mid \mathbf{y})\) consistent observed data.  Formally, Bayesian p-value test statistic \(T\) : $$   p_B = \Pr\bigl(T(\mathbf{y}^{\mathrm{rep}}) \ge T(\mathbf{y})         \mid \mathbf{y}\bigr), $$ near \(0.5\) well-specified model near \(0\) \(1\) systematic misspecification (Gelman et al., 1996). zero rate \(T_0\) share \(T_s\) chosen directly correspond two structural parts hurdle model.  defect \(T_0\) indicates misspecification Bernoulli participation equation; defect \(T_s\) indicates misspecification zero-truncated Beta-Binomial intensity equation.  Together provide minimal targeted diagnostic toolkit, following visualisation philosophy Gabry et al. (2019).

title: Posterior Predictive Checks for Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models — ppc
text: test statistic "passes" PPC level \(\alpha\) observed value falls within \((1-\alpha)\) central posterior predictive interval: $$   T(\mathbf{y}) \;\\;   \bigl[Q_{\alpha/2}\bigl(T(\mathbf{y}^{\mathrm{rep}})\bigr),\;          Q_{1-\alpha/2}\bigl(T(\mathbf{y}^{\mathrm{rep}})\bigr)   \bigr]. $$ Coverage reported coverage element returned object.

title: Posterior Predictive Checks for Hurdle Beta-Binomial Models — ppc
text: "stan" Extracts posterior predictive replications \(\mathbf{y}^{\mathrm{rep}}\) directly y_rep[N] array Stan generated quantities block.  preferred method: uses exact joint posterior incurs additional R-side simulation cost. "simulate" Reconstructs \(\mathbf{y}^{\mathrm{rep}}\) R composing posterior draws \((\alpha, \beta, \log\kappa)\) hurdle Beta-Binomial PMF via rhurdle_betabinom.  Useful cross-validating Stan GQ block models GQ draws unavailable.

what: Predictions from a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — predict.hbb_fit
title: Predictions from a Hurdle Beta-Binomial Model — predict.hbb_fit
text: Point predictions computed posterior mean fixed effects (state random effects SVC models): $$   \hat{y}_i = \hat{q}(x_i) \cdot \hat\mu(x_i), $$ \(\hat{q}(x) = \mathrm{logistic}(x'\hat\alpha)\) \(\hat\mu(x) = \mathrm{logistic}(x'\hat\beta)\). interval = "credible", full posterior distribution propagated link function produce empirical credible intervals.  MCMC draw \(m\): $$   \hat{y}_i^{(m)} = q^{(m)}(x_i) \cdot \mu^{(m)}(x_i), $$ \((\alpha/2,\; 1-\alpha/2)\) quantiles across draws give interval bounds.

code: # Minimal example with the small synthetic dataset data(nsece_synth_small, package = "hurdlebb") f <- hbb_formula(y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban) d <- prepare_stan_data(f, nsece_synth_small) d #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Data #> ------------------------- #>   Observations (N) : 504  #>   Covariates   (P) : 3 (intercept + 2 predictors)  #>   Groups       (S) : 1  #>   Policy vars  (Q) : 1  #>   Positive obs     : 313 (62.1%)  #>   Zero rate        : 0.379  #>   RE dimension (K) : 0  #>   Model type       : base  #>   Survey weights   : no #>   X standardised   : yes  # With grouping and weights f2 <- hbb_formula(   y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban + (1 | state_id) ) d2 <- prepare_stan_data(f2, nsece_synth_small, weights = "weight") d2 #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Data #> ------------------------- #>   Observations (N) : 504  #>   Covariates   (P) : 3 (intercept + 2 predictors)  #>   Groups       (S) : 51  #>   Policy vars  (Q) : 1  #>   Positive obs     : 313 (62.1%)  #>   Zero rate        : 0.379  #>   RE dimension (K) : 2  #>   Model type       : weighted  #>   Survey weights   : yes (range 0.05--14.92)  #>   X standardised   : yes  # Full SVC model with policy moderators data(nsece_state_policy, package = "hurdlebb") f3 <- hbb_formula(   y | trials(n_trial) ~ poverty + urban +     (poverty + urban | state_id) +     state_level(mr_pctile) ) d3 <- prepare_stan_data(   f3, nsece_synth_small,   weights = "weight", state_data = nsece_state_policy ) d3 #> Hurdle Beta-Binomial Data #> ------------------------- #>   Observations (N) : 504  #>   Covariates   (P) : 3 (intercept + 2 predictors)  #>   Groups       (S) : 51  #>   Policy vars  (Q) : 2  #>   Positive obs     : 313 (62.1%)  #>   Zero rate        : 0.379  #>   RE dimension (K) : 6  #>   Model type       : svc_weighted  #>   Survey weights   : yes (range 0.05--14.92)  #>   X standardised   : yes

title: Residuals from an hbb_fit — residuals.hbb_fit
text: "response" Raw residuals proportion scale: \(r_i = y_i / n_i - \hat{q}_i \hat{\mu}_i\). "pearson" Pearson residuals standardised model-implied standard deviation (count scale): $$       r_i^P = \frac{y_i - n_i \hat{q}_i \hat{\mu}_i}                    {\sqrt{\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(Y_i)}},     $$ \(\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(Y_i)\) computed via hurdle_variance using \((n_i, \hat{q}_i,     \hat\mu_i, \hat\kappa)\). correct model specification, Pearson residuals approximately mean zero variance one.

title: Residuals from an hbb_fit — residuals.hbb_fit
text: Several guards applied prevent division zero NaN: \(\hat\mu_i\) clamped \((\varepsilon,\; 1 - \varepsilon)\) \(\varepsilon = \sqrt{\mathtt{.Machine\$double.eps}}\) (\(\approx 1.49 \times 10^{-8}\)). \(\hat{q}_i\) clamped \([\varepsilon,\; 1]\).  (\(q = 0\) yields \(\mathrm{Var}[Y] = 0\), making Pearson undefined.) \(\hat{\kappa}\) clamped \(\le 10^{15}\). hurdle variance floored .Machine$double.eps prevent division zero. Non-finite residuals replaced NA warning issued.

title: Cluster-Robust Sandwich Variance Estimator — sandwich_variance
text: Computes design-corrected sandwich variance-covariance matrix fixed effects survey-weighted hurdle Beta-Binomial model. estimator uses stratified cluster-robust formula

title: Cluster-Robust Sandwich Variance Estimator — sandwich_variance
text: fit object class "hbb_fit", returned hbb(). Must weighted model (model_type %% c("weighted", "svc_weighted")), since score generated quantities produced survey-weighted Stan models.

title: Cluster-Robust Sandwich Variance Estimator — sandwich_variance
text: S3 object class "hbb_sandwich" containing: V_sand Numeric \(D \times D\) sandwich variance matrix. H_obs Numeric \(D \times D\) observed Fisher information (block-diagonal: extensive \(P \times P\) intensive+kappa \((P+1) \times (P+1)\)). H_obs_inv Numeric \(D \times D\) inverse H_obs, representing data-variance (design correction). J_cluster Numeric \(D \times D\) cluster-robust meat matrix. Sigma_MCMC Numeric \(D \times D\) posterior covariance fixed-effect draws (comparison, used bread). scores Numeric \(N \times D\) posterior mean score matrix. DER Named numeric vector length \(D\). Design Effect Ratios: \(\mathrm{DER}_p = V_{\mathrm{sand}}[p,p] /     H_{\mathrm{obs}}^{-1}[p,p]\). param_labels Character vector length \(D\) human-readable parameter labels. D Integer. Total fixed-effect dimension. N Integer. Number observations. P Integer. Number covariates (including intercept). model_type Character. model type fit. survey_info List survey design summaries: n_strata, n_psu, df, n_singleton. matrix_diagnostics List eigenvalue/condition diagnostics H_obs, J_cluster, V_sand, Sigma_MCMC. nearPD_applied Logical. Whether nearPD correction applied V_sand. call matched call.

title: Cluster-Robust Sandwich Variance Estimator — sandwich_variance
text: $$V_{\mathrm{sand}} = H_{\mathrm{obs}}^{-1}\, J_{\mathrm{cluster}}\,       H_{\mathrm{obs}}^{-1}$$ \(H_{\mathrm{obs}}\) block-diagonal observed Fisher information (analytic extensive margin, empirical information identity intensive margin plus dispersion), \(J_{\mathrm{cluster}}\) cluster-robust "meat" matrix accounts within-PSU correlation unequal survey weights.

title: Cluster-Robust Sandwich Variance Estimator — sandwich_variance
text: hierarchical models state random effects, MCMC posterior covariance fixed effects absorbs prior random-effect variance, leading \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}} \gg H_{\mathrm{obs}}^{-1}\). Using \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) bread yields astronomical Design Effect Ratios (3000–26000 NSECE application). explicit \(H_{\mathrm{obs}}\) resolves .

title: Cluster-Robust Sandwich Variance Estimator — sandwich_variance
text: \(D = 2P + 1\) dimensional parameter vector ordered $$\theta = (\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_P,\; \beta_1, \ldots,       \beta_P,\; \log\kappa)$$ \(\alpha\) governs extensive margin (Bernoulli), \(\beta\) governs intensive margin (zero-truncated Beta-Binomial), \(\kappa\) dispersion parameter.

title: Cluster-Robust Sandwich Variance Estimator — sandwich_variance
text: DER parameter \(\mathrm{DER}_p = V_{\mathrm{sand}}[p,p] / H_{\mathrm{obs}}^{-1}[p,p]\). Expected values 1–5 typical survey designs.

title: Cluster-Robust Sandwich Variance Estimator — sandwich_variance
text: Williams, M. R. Savitsky, T. D. (2021). Uncertainty estimation pseudo-Bayesian inference complex sampling. International Statistical Review, 89(1), 72–107.

title: Cluster-Robust Sandwich Variance Estimator — sandwich_variance

title: hurdlebb 0.1.0
text: Initial release. Two-part hurdle beta-binomial model via CmdStan. Four model variants: base, weighted, SVC, SVC + weighted. Survey-weighted pseudo-posterior inference. Sandwich variance estimation Cholesky posterior recalibration. Average marginal effects extensive/intensive margin decomposition. LOO-CV model comparison posterior predictive checks. Three built-synthetic datasets based NSECE 2019. Five vignettes covering full analysis workflow.
